error: He says, “Call her anything else and she’d come back.”

story:
We came down South for Grandma’s funeral. Daddy’s sleeping on the couch, Mama’s in the little bed, and me and my brother Richard have to sleep in Grandma’s room, which we never did without her. Grandma was Daddy’s mama, but his Aunt Chloe—who lives in Chicago with us and is rich and fun and takes us to free concerts—raised him. Daddy calls Aunt Chloe “Mama” and used to call Grandma “Ma’am.”

It’s country-dark in Grandma’s room, the kind of dark we don’t get in the city. No streetlights, no prayer candle like when Grandma was alive. Me and Richard lie in the bed with one pillow, listening. Then we hear scratching—close, like right behind the headboard. Richard’s five and gets scared easy, especially since the funeral. He thinks dying is like catching a cold and keeps asking how not to die. I tell him it’s the wind, then a tree branch, then maybe bugs. He doesn’t believe me. I try to get up to pull the light string, but I can’t see my own feet. Something brushes my toes and feels like it’s feeling me back. I jump onto the bed and tell him the country’s full of bugs. The scratching climbs higher. Richard wants to scream so Daddy will come and turn on the light. I don’t want a whipping, but the sound keeps coming. We both call, “Daddy!”

The door opens, but the light isn’t from the hall. It’s coming from her. Grandma steps in glowing like one of her prayer candles. Richard whispers that the cemetery people put her in the ground, so why is she here? My arm hurts where he’s pinching me. Grandma moves to the bed with that soft light and tells Richard not to pee on her new mattress—it cost $375. I’m the one who almost does. Then she says she’s got a kitten under the bed. The runt, sickly, hasn’t meowed yet. We’re going to take care of her; if we don’t, she’ll come back and we won’t like it. She pulls out a tiny white kitten with one black spot and tosses her into our bed. “I named her Chloe after my sister,” Grandma says. “Call her anything else and I will come back.” The door slams; the light disappears. I’m holding the warm little cat against my chest. Richard stops crying.

The door opens again, regular hallway light this time. Daddy comes in, asking why we aren’t sleeping. We tell him Grandma was here and gave us a kitten named Chloe. He says, “Call her anything else and she’d come back.” He squints and asks if we ate crab apples. We did, but that doesn’t explain the cat. He pulls the string; the light comes on. The kitten’s real—snow white with a black dot between her eyes. He says we’re lucky she’s cute; Mama might let us keep her if we keep the ghost part to ourselves. He leaves the door halfway open so a slice of light stays with us.

Richard asks if we can still get a puppy. I say I hope so because you can’t play fetch with a baby cat. I toss Chloe into his lap. The room goes black-dark again, and a voice—Grandma’s—says she’s not playing with me and I’d better take care of her kitten. The light returns. Richard sits there petting Chloe and grinning. “And don’t forget, her name is Chloe,” he says.
